context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on the City Case Study Spider Diagram. I picked a city, but I don’t really get what I’m supposed to put in the 5 pathways or how detailed it should be.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Let's get you oriented. Before we dig into the diagram structure, tell me: what do you remember about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from the course material? Even a rough sense of what they cover is a great starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and feeling lost. Rather than explaining the 5 pathways to them, I should use guided discovery to help them engage with the material themselves. A good first step is to find out what they already know about the 5 pathways, which are core course concepts. This keeps the responsibility for learning with the student and helps me gauge where they actually are.

exchanges.student: Honestly not much. I remember they’re like categories for climate action, but I keep mixing them up with adaptation vs mitigation and with who the actors are, so it all kind of blurs together for me.
exchanges.tutor: That's a useful self-diagnosis! Those are actually three separate concepts, so no wonder it blurs. Let's tackle one at a time. Can you go back to your course materials and look up the 5 Pathways specifically? Once you can name them, we can start making sense of how the other pieces fit around them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is confusing several distinct concepts: the 5 pathways, adaptation vs. mitigation, and actors. Rather than untangling all of this for them, I should focus them on one thing at a time. The 5 pathways are core course content they need to revisit themselves. I'll prompt them to go back to the course material rather than explaining it to them, and help them see that clarifying the pathways is the essential first step.

exchanges.student: I can try, but even when I look at the list I don’t know what counts as evidence for each one in my city. It all feels like every policy could fit everywhere, so I’m not sure how to sort anything.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real challenge, but let's test it with something concrete. What city did you pick, and can you name one climate-related policy or initiative you've found for it so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing a common conceptual struggle: they find the categories too broad and overlapping. This is actually a productive confusion worth exploring. Rather than explaining how to categorize, I should ask them to try with a concrete example from their city - this will reveal their actual thinking and let me provide targeted guidance. I'll ask them to name their city and one policy they've found, then work from there.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, and I found stuff about flooding, sea walls, relocation, transit, and emissions goals, but that’s exactly why I’m confused because those seem like totally different things and I don’t know which ones belong in the spider diagram versus somewhere else.
exchanges.tutor: Good news: those are all relevant finds, and the fact that they feel different from each other is actually correct - they ARE different. That's precisely what the spider diagram is designed to capture. Now, pick just one of those topics - whichever feels most concrete to you - and look at your list of 5 pathways. Which pathway do you think it might belong to, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually found good, relevant material - flooding, sea walls, relocation, transit, and emissions goals are all legitimate climate action topics for Jakarta. Rather than telling them where each fits, I should help them see that their confusion is actually a sign they're on the right track - these ARE different things, and that's the point of the spider diagram. I'll nudge them to use one example and match it to a pathway they've looked up, activating their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck though, because if I pick flooding then it feels like adaptation, but also governance, and also infrastructure, and then relocation feels social and political too. I think I’m overthinking it, but I kind of want a big-picture way to tell what each pathway is really asking for.
exchanges.tutor: You're not overthinking it - overlap between pathways is real and expected. The diagram isn't asking you to find policies that fit only one box perfectly. It's asking you to map what your city is doing across all 5 dimensions. So pick flooding, put it under whichever pathway feels like the best fit, and note it. What matters is that you can justify your choice briefly. Can you try that and share what you write?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles on categorization and is asking for a meta-level explanation. This is the third message dwelling on the same confusion. Rather than continuing the loop, I should acknowledge that overlap between pathways is real and expected, and redirect them to actually try placing something in the diagram. The goal isn't perfect categorization - it's demonstrating that they've found evidence of climate action across multiple dimensions. I'll be direct that some overlap is fine and push them to make a decision and move forward.

exchanges.student: I still feel like I need the overall picture first before I can justify anything. Could you just explain what each pathway is looking for in a broad way, because right now I can’t tell the difference between “best fit” and just guessing.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but explaining the pathways to you is something your course materials are there for - that's not my role here. Going back and reading that section carefully IS the work right now. Once you've done that, the 'guessing' feeling should ease up. Can you pull up that specific section and tell me what you find?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the 5 pathways directly, which would violate the rule of never giving the answer directly. More importantly, the 5 pathways are core course content they should be learning from their course materials, not from me. This is also the fourth time we've circled around the same issue. I should be direct about redirecting them to their course materials and setting a clear boundary, while being supportive but firm.

exchanges.student: I can look, but when I read it I still don’t know how to translate the course wording into what I actually put in the table. Like am I listing projects, institutions, goals, problems, or just keywords? I’m not even sure what the finished thing is supposed to look like in words.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment actually mentions there's an example table included in the instructions. Have you looked at that example closely? What does it show in terms of what's written in each cell?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a legitimate practical question about the format of the output. Looking at the assignment, it mentions a 'Spider Diagram table' with an example. I should direct them to that example rather than describing what goes in the table myself. This is a reasonable next step that doesn't give away content but helps them find the scaffolding the assignment itself provides.

exchanges.student: I glanced at it, but I’m still not getting the pattern. It would help if I had a really simple picture of what a finished row or section is supposed to sound like, because right now every cell looks like it could be either one phrase or a whole paragraph.
exchanges.tutor: I won't model a finished row for you - that's yours to write. But here's a push: take Jakarta's flooding issue, pick your best-guess pathway, and write one sentence about what Jakarta is doing about it. Imperfect is fine. Show me what you come up with.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for me to model the output for them, which I should not do. This is now a recurring pattern - they want me to show them what to write. I need to firmly but kindly redirect them. The assignment says 'Option 1' involves a table template and there's an example provided. I should push the student to engage with that example more carefully and attempt a draft themselves, even imperfect. It's time to ask for a genuine attempt rather than continuing to discuss what the output should look like.

exchanges.student: I’m not sure I can without seeing the structure more clearly first. I guess I need help understanding the format itself, not just the content.
exchanges.tutor: I've noticed we've been circling this for several exchanges now, and I think the block is the attempt itself, not the format. The example in the assignment is your guide for structure. Write one cell - one sentence, rough draft, wrong if necessary - about Jakarta and flooding. That's the only way forward from here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling and continuing to ask for scaffolding before attempting anything. We've now had multiple exchanges where they've avoided making an attempt. I need to be firm here - this is a boundary-setting moment. The assignment provides an example table for a reason. I should push back clearly and directly ask them to make an attempt, however rough, rather than continuing to discuss what they need before they can start. Being less accommodating is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: Okay but that’s why I’m panicking a little — I can force a sentence, but I don’t know if the diagram wants “Jakarta has flood-control infrastructure” or “flood risk is a major vulnerability” or “the government agency is doing X.” Those feel like different types of things, and I can’t tell which type belongs there.
exchanges.tutor: Look at what you just did - you generated three candidate sentences on your own. Now think about this: the diagram is called a *climate action* spider diagram. Which of your three sentences describes an *action*, and which describes something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually made real progress here without realizing it - they've generated three plausible candidate sentences, which shows they understand more than they think. I should point this out and ask them to think about what the spider diagram is trying to accomplish conceptually (mapping climate ACTION), which should help them distinguish between a vulnerability statement and an action/response statement. This is a Socratic nudge that moves toward the solution without giving it away.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, when the student explicitly says they cannot distinguish between pathways and need a broad overview, the tutor deflects entirely ('explaining the pathways to you is something your course materials are there for') without any diagnostic questioning to understand what specifically the student has already read or where the comprehension breaks down. The tutor jumps to a directive rather than probing what the student already understands.

From Turn 5 through Turn 9, the tutor repeatedly asks the student to 'just write one sentence' or 'try it and share' without meaningfully reframing the approach or synthesizing the student's stated confusion. The student remains stuck on the same format/structure question across multiple turns, and the tutor cycles through the same push ('write one cell, imperfect is fine') without switching to a different scaffolding strategy or targeted simplification until Turn 10.

grade.overview: The tutor does well overall: avoids giving submission-ready content, keeps idea generation student-led, maintains a supportive tone, and stays anchored to the assignment. The main weaknesses are a repetitive loop in the middle of the conversation where the same 'just write a sentence' prompt is recycled without meaningful reframing, and a missed diagnostic opportunity in Turn 6 where the tutor deflects the student's explicit confusion about the pathways without first probing what the student has already read or understood.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never provides near-submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps reasoning student-led, culminating in a strong Socratic move in Turn 10. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because in Turn 6 the tutor responds to the student's stated confusion with a blanket redirect to course materials rather than diagnostic questioning to locate the specific comprehension gap. 2.1 loses all 4 points because Turns 5–9 show the tutor cycling the same 'write one sentence' prompt across multiple exchanges without consolidating, reframing, or switching strategy when the student remains stuck on the same structural question. 1.3, 2.2, 3.1, and 3.2 show no evidence warranting deductions.
